% Title and authors
\title{Adaptive Per-Distance Sampling for Efficient Multi-Scale Network Centrality Analysis}
\author{%
  Author Name\\
  \textit{Affiliation}\\
  \texttt{email@example.com}
}
\date{\today}

\begin{document}

\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
  Localised network centrality, computed across multiple distance thresholds, characterises accessibility and flow in street networks. For large street graphs, however, the cost is often effectively quadratic in network size, making multi-scale analysis prohibitive. Sampling-based approximation can reduce this cost, yet existing sample-complexity results target global centrality on general graphs and do not directly address the localised setting. We observe that for distance-bounded centrality estimated from sampled source nodes, accuracy depends primarily on the number of nodes reachable within the distance threshold rather than on total network size. Building on this insight, we develop a two-parameter adaptive model that calibrates sampling separately for each distance threshold based on mean reach, and cross-validate it against a localised adaptation of the Eppstein--Wang error bound. We validate the approach on the Greater London and Greater Madrid street networks at distances from 1--20\,km, achieving Spearman $\rho > \glaMinRho$ for both harmonic closeness and betweenness centrality with speedups of \glaFiveKmSpeedup{}--\glaTwentyKmSpeedup{}$\times$. The approach is implemented in the open-source \cityseer{} Python package.
\end{abstract}

\textbf{Keywords:} network centrality, sampling, urban morphology, betweenness, harmonic closeness, computational efficiency
